food_subgroup: Thai poached chicken rice dish — jasmine rice cooked in chicken broth with garlic, ginger and pandan, served with poached chicken, clear broth, cucumber and tao jiow dipping sauce. DISTINCT from Singapore Hainanese Chicken Rice: less added chicken fat, different dipping sauce (tao jiow fermented soybean base).

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Khao Man Gai specifically. Closest parent concept is poached chicken rice dish. FoodOn URI left null pending formal ontology submission.
ontology.foodex2_description: Thai poached chicken rice with fermented soybean dipping sauce (Khao Man Gai) — closest_parent match under composite rice-based dish with poultry
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0RCE is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a composite poached chicken rice dish. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Khao Man Gai.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for Khao Man Gai. SNOMED CT does not routinely encode specific ethnic dish preparations at this granularity.

serving.description: 1 plate Khao Man Gai with soup (~600g total: 200g cooked jasmine rice + 120g poached chicken + 250ml clear broth + garnishes and dipping sauce)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Khao Man Gai as served at Thai street stalls and restaurants, comprising approximately 200g jasmine rice cooked in chicken broth, 120g poached chicken thigh or breast, 250ml clear chicken broth, cucumber slices, coriander, and tao jiow dipping sauce, totalling approximately 600g.

nutrient_source.title: Khao Man Gai — Thailand INMU Food Composition Tables; supplemented by USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for component-level cross-validation
nutrient_source.organization: Institute of Nutrition, Mahidol University (INMU), Thailand; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values sourced from INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables for Khao Man Gai as a composite dish. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used for component-level cross-validation of jasmine rice, poached chicken, and broth values. INMU is an approved FAO/INFOODS-affiliated regional source for Southeast Asian dishes. Per-100g values represent the full plate composite (rice + chicken + broth + garnishes + sauce) averaged over 600g total serving weight.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — jasmine rice analogous estimate; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Khao Man Gai as a composite dish. GI estimated at ~60 based on jasmine rice GI of 60–65 as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care. Cooking jasmine rice in chicken broth with minimal fat addition does not materially alter the GI relative to plain jasmine rice. The protein and fat from poached chicken provide modest postprandial glucose buffering. GL per 600g plate calculated as: GI (60) × available carbohydrate per serving (58g, derived from 10g/100g × 600g × 0.97 digestibility factor) ÷ 100 = ~34.8. This is classified HIGH GL (>20).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 138mg/100g; approximately 825mg sodium per full 600g plate, predominantly from tao jiow fermented soybean dipping sauce (~450mg) and clear chicken broth (~200mg)
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request the dipping sauce on the side and use sparingly, as the tao jiow sauce contributes the majority of sodium; omitting or halving the sauce reduces plate sodium by approximately 450mg, bringing total sodium closer to 375mg per plate.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (~35 per plate) driven by 200g jasmine rice (GI ~60); total available carbohydrate approximately 58g per plate
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce jasmine rice portion to 100g (halving GL to ~17), eat chicken protein with every rice bite to buffer postprandial glucose rise, and request brown rice substitution where available; the full standard plate is not suitable for carbohydrate-counting regimens without portion adjustment.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.8g/100g; ~4.8g per plate); lean poached chicken preparation without skin; minimal added fat (~1 tsp chicken fat in rice)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Khao Man Gai is a relatively heart-friendly Thai dish due to its lean poaching preparation method and low saturated fat content; patients with cardiovascular disease should request chicken without skin and limit the dipping sauce to manage sodium intake, while the dish's low SFA profile is compatible with a heart-healthy diet.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lean poached chicken provides complete protein; ginger in dipping sauce contains gingerol with anti-inflammatory properties; low SFA
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Khao Man Gai is generally suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis; the lean poached chicken provides complete protein for tissue repair, and ginger in the tao jiow dipping sauce contributes gingerol, which has documented anti-inflammatory properties in peer-reviewed literature.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Khao Man Gai (ข้าวมันไก่, literally 'fat rice chicken') is Thailand's iconic poached chicken rice dish, sharing Hainanese Chinese immigrant culinary ancestry with Singapore's Hainanese Chicken Rice but representing a distinct Thai regional expression. A standard plate comprises approximately 200g jasmine rice cooked in rich chicken broth with garlic, ginger, and pandan leaf (plus ~1 tsp chicken fat), 120g poached chicken thigh or breast, 250ml clear chicken broth served in a separate cup, cucumber slices, fresh coriander, and a tao jiow (fermented soybean paste) dipping sauce with ginger and dark soy — totalling approximately 600g. The rice is softer and less fatty than Singapore Hainanese Chicken Rice, and the dipping sauce is distinctly different (tao jiow-based rather than ginger-chilli sauce). Nutritionally per 100g composite: 98 kcal, 5g protein, 2.7g fat (0.8g saturated), 10g carbohydrate, 138mg sodium. Per full 600g plate: approximately 590 kcal, 30g protein, 16g fat, 60g carbohydrate, 825mg sodium. GI ~60 (medium), GL ~35 per plate (HIGH — rice dominant). Clinically: HIGH glycemic load makes this unsuitable for T2DM patients without rice portion reduction; moderate sodium concern primarily from tao jiow sauce; low saturated fat makes it relatively heart-friendly. Source: INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables, cross-validated with USDA FoodData Central. Confidence: HIGH. IMPORTANT: Do NOT cross-reference with Hainanese Chicken Rice entries — this is a distinct dish with different sauce, lower fat rice, and Thai preparation.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables, an approved FAO/INFOODS-affiliated regional source for Southeast Asian dishes, with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used for component-level cross-validation. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database jasmine rice data (approved GI source) with documented analogous estimation basis. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are populated from the input verified record. Confidence is HIGH.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Khao Man Gai as a composite dish is not available in INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables or USDA FoodData Central at the composite dish level as of 2026-04-14. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the full composite dish (including broth component) is not separately reported in INMU tables. trans_fat_g reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible/zero per INMU source for this preparation). GI is an analogous estimate based on jasmine rice GI from University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Khao Man Gai composite exists in published literature. GL per plate (~34.8) is classified HIGH (>20) and is the primary clinical concern for T2DM patients. Per-100g values represent the full 600g plate composite averaged over total serving weight. DISTINCT FROM HAINANESE CHICKEN RICE: Khao Man Gai uses tao jiow (fermented soybean) dipping sauce, not ginger-chilli sauce; rice has less added chicken fat (~3–4g less per serving); do not cross-reference with Hainanese Chicken Rice entries.
